title: Standard Startup Procedure for RDX Digital Textile Printer
content:
Before starting the machine, ensure that the main power supply is stable and connected through the
approved power stabilizer . Check that the emergency stop button is released and that no tools, cloth pieces,
or loose objects are present on the print bed or carriage path.
Turn on the main power switch and wait for the control panel to initialize. Once the software interface is
loaded, check the machine status indicators. The air pressure should be within the recommended operating
range, and the ink tank levels should be above the minimum level.
Start the ink circulation system before enabling printhead firing. Allow the system to circulate ink for at
least three minutes. After circulation, perform a nozzle test to confirm that all printheads are firing
correctly. If missing lines appear in the nozzle test, perform cleaning before production printing.
Do not begin printing until the machine status shows Ready.

title: Standard Shutdown Procedure for RDX Digital Textile Printer
content:
At the end of the production shift, stop all active print jobs from the machine software. Move the carriage to
the home position using the control panel. Do not manually push the carriage unless instructed by service
personnel.
Run a short ink circulation cycle before shutdown to prevent ink settling inside the lines. After circulation,
clean the printhead surface using approved lint-free wipes and cleaning solution. Avoid applying excessive
pressure on the printhead nozzle plate.
Check the capping station and ensure that the printheads are properly seated. Improper capping may
cause nozzle drying and print quality problems during the next startup.
Turn off the print software, then switch off the machine controller . Finally, turn off the main power switch.
The machine should not be disconnected from power abruptly unless there is an emergency.

title: How to Perform a Printhead Nozzle Test
content:
A nozzle test must be performed before starting production and whenever print quality issues appear . Load
the test fabric or test sheet on the print bed and ensure it is flat without wrinkles.
Open the machine software and select the nozzle test option from the maintenance menu. Choose the
printhead group to be tested. Start the test and observe the printed pattern carefully.
A correct nozzle test should show continuous and evenly spaced lines for each channel. Missing lines,
broken lines, or uneven firing indicate nozzle blockage or air bubbles in the ink path.
If minor missing lines are visible, perform light cleaning and repeat the nozzle test. If several lines are
missing, perform medium cleaning and check ink circulation. Do not perform repeated heavy cleaning
unnecessarily, as it may waste ink and stress the printhead.
Production printing should begin only after the nozzle test is acceptable.

title: Ink Loading and Ink Tank Handling Instructions
content:
Use only approved textile pigment ink recommended for the machine. Before loading ink, confirm the color
label on the ink container and match it with the corresponding ink tank.
Shake the ink container gently before filling. Do not shake aggressively, as this may create foam or air
bubbles. Open the ink tank cap carefully and fill ink slowly to avoid spillage.
The ink level should remain between the minimum and maximum markings on the tank. Operating the
machine with very low ink levels may allow air to enter the ink line and cause nozzle dropout.
After filling ink, close the tank cap firmly. Wipe any spilled ink immediately using approved cleaning
material. Run ink circulation for two to three minutes after refilling.
Never mix different ink brands or expired ink in the same tank. Mixing incompatible inks may cause
clogging, sediment formation, or color inconsistency.
2

title: Fabric Loading Guidelines for Flatbed Textile Printing
content:
Before loading fabric, make sure the print bed surface is clean and free from dust, dried ink, adhesive
residue, and loose threads. The fabric must be properly pre-treated and dried according to production
requirements.
Place the fabric evenly on the print bed. Smooth the fabric from the center outward to remove wrinkles.
Ensure that the fabric edges do not interfere with the carriage movement.
For stretchable fabrics, avoid excessive pulling because it may distort the print after unloading. Use proper
adhesive or holding method based on the fabric type.
Set the correct print origin in the software before starting the job. Confirm that the design placement
matches the fabric position. A test print is recommended when using a new fabric type or size.
Do not print on wet, dusty, or uneven fabric, as this may affect print sharpness and printhead safety.

title: Adjusting Print Bed Height Before Printing
content:
Correct print bed height is important for print quality and printhead safety. Before printing, check the
thickness of the fabric or garment placed on the bed.
Use the bed height adjustment controls to set the correct distance between the printhead and the fabric
surface. The gap should be close enough for sharp printing but not so close that the fabric touches the
printhead.
If the bed is too low, the print may appear blurry or misty. If the bed is too high, the fabric may strike the
printhead and cause serious damage.
After adjustment, move the carriage slowly across the print area using manual controls and confirm that
there is no contact with the fabric. Pay special attention to seams, collars, folded edges, and thick areas.
Do not leave loose fabric parts raised above the print surface.
3

title: Daily Cleaning Procedure for Textile Printer
content:
Daily cleaning must be performed before and after production. Start by cleaning the print bed surface using
a soft cloth. Remove fabric lint, thread pieces, dust, and adhesive residue.
Inspect the carriage path and remove any obstruction. Clean the encoder strip gently using approved
cleaning solution if dust or ink mist is visible. Do not scratch or bend the encoder strip.
Clean the printhead bottom surface with lint-free wipes and approved solution. Wipe in one direction only.
Avoid rubbing the nozzle plate repeatedly.
Check the capping station, wiper blade, and waste ink tray. Remove dried ink deposits carefully. Make sure
the waste ink bottle has enough space and is not overflowing.
Daily cleaning helps maintain stable print quality and reduces nozzle blockage. Skipping cleaning may cause
print defects, carriage movement errors, and unnecessary downtime.

title: Weekly Maintenance Checklist
content:
Weekly maintenance should be performed by a trained operator or maintenance technician. Begin by
checking all visible cables, connectors, ink tubes, and air tubes for looseness, leakage, or damage.
Inspect the belt tension and carriage movement. The carriage should move smoothly without unusual
vibration or sound. Check the linear guide rail and apply approved lubrication if required.
Clean the air filter and inspect the cooling fans. Poor airflow may cause electronic components to overheat
during long production runs.
Check the ink circulation system for stable flow. Confirm that no bubbles are visible in the ink lines. Inspect
the capping station alignment and wiper condition.
Review the software error log for repeated warnings. Repeated errors should be reported before they
become major failures.
Record all weekly maintenance activities in the machine maintenance log.
4

title: Emergency Stop Button Usage and Safety
content:
The emergency stop button must be used only when immediate machine stoppage is required. Examples
include fabric collision, carriage obstruction, electrical smell, smoke, abnormal noise, or risk of operator
injury.
Pressing the emergency stop button immediately cuts machine motion and stops active operation. After
pressing it, do not restart the machine until the cause of the emergency is identified and corrected.
To reset the emergency stop, rotate or release the button as per the machine design. Then restart the
control system from the panel. Check the software status and clear any active alarms.
Do not use the emergency stop button as a normal pause or stop control. Frequent unnecessary use may
interrupt system processes and cause job failure.
All operators must know the location and function of the emergency stop button before using the machine.

title: Electrical Power Supply Requirements
content:
The textile printer must be connected to a stable power source as specified by the manufacturer . A suitable
power stabilizer or UPS is recommended to protect sensitive electronics from voltage fluctuations.
Before installation, verify voltage, phase, grounding, and load capacity. Poor grounding may cause
communication errors, electrical noise, or risk of shock.
Do not connect heavy external equipment to the same power line used by the printer . Sudden load changes
may affect printer stability during operation.
The machine should not be switched on and off repeatedly within a short period. Wait at least one minute
before restarting after power-off.
If frequent power failure occurs, stop production and safely shut down the machine. Unexpected power loss
during printing may result in job failure, fabric wastage, or printhead maintenance issues.
Electrical work must be handled only by qualified personnel.
5

title: Loading a Print Job in Machine Software
content:
Open the machine software and confirm that the printer status is Ready. Import the design file in the
supported format. Check the file resolution, dimensions, color profile, and print placement before sending
the job.
Select the correct machine profile and print mode based on the fabric type and required output quality.
Higher resolution modes may improve detail but will increase production time.
Set the print origin according to the fabric position on the bed. Preview the design to confirm that it is
within the printable area.
Before printing a new design, perform a small test print or sample print. Confirm color accuracy, sharpness,
and alignment.
Do not modify machine parameters randomly during active printing. Changes to speed, pass count, or ink
limit should be done only after understanding their effect on output quality.

title: Selecting the Correct Print Mode
content:
Print mode selection affects print speed, ink usage, and output quality. For high-speed bulk production,
standard mode can be used when the design does not require very fine detail.
For detailed artwork, gradients, skin tones, or premium samples, high-quality mode is recommended. High-
quality mode uses more passes and may require more production time.
For dark fabrics, confirm that the white ink layer and color layer settings are correct. Incorrect white ink
settings may result in dull colors or poor opacity.
Before confirming a print mode, check fabric type, design complexity, customer quality requirement, and
delivery timeline.
Operators should avoid changing advanced print parameters without approval. Incorrect settings can cause
banding, over-inking, color mismatch, or poor wash durability.
Record successful print settings for repeat orders.
6

title: Ink Circulation System Overview
content:
The ink circulation system helps keep ink moving through the ink lines and prevents settling. This is
especially important for white ink and pigment inks, which may contain particles that settle over time.
The circulation system should be started before printhead firing. During long idle periods, circulation helps
maintain ink consistency and reduces clogging risk.
Check the ink lines regularly for air bubbles, leakage, or abnormal flow. Air bubbles may cause missing
nozzles, unstable printing, or sudden color dropout.
If circulation noise becomes abnormal or ink flow appears weak, stop production and inspect the pump,
filter , and ink line connections.
Do not run the circulation system without sufficient ink in the tank. Running with low ink may introduce air
into the system.
Follow the maintenance schedule for filter replacement.

title: White Ink Handling and Maintenance
content:
White ink requires special attention because it may settle faster than other colors. Before starting
production, ensure that the white ink circulation system has run for the recommended duration.
Gently agitate or circulate white ink according to the machine procedure. Do not allow white ink to remain
idle for long periods without circulation.
Perform a nozzle test for white channels before printing on dark garments. Missing white nozzles may
reduce opacity and affect the final color output.
If white ink appears weak, uneven, or grainy, check ink level, circulation flow, filter condition, and nozzle
status.
Do not mix old and new white ink from different batches unless approved. Always close the ink tank cap
after filling to prevent contamination.
White ink maintenance should be recorded daily.
7

title: Print Alignment and Registration Procedure
content:
Print alignment must be checked when installing the machine, replacing printheads, changing print modes,
or observing shadowing in printed output.
Use the alignment test pattern available in the machine software. Print the test pattern on a stable test
fabric or sheet. Review horizontal and vertical alignment marks carefully.
If the printed lines do not overlap correctly, adjust alignment values in the software. Make small changes
and repeat the test until the alignment is acceptable.
Incorrect alignment may cause blurry edges, color shadows, double lines, or reduced image sharpness.
Do not adjust mechanical parts unless software alignment cannot correct the issue. Mechanical adjustment
should be performed only by trained service personnel.
Save successful alignment settings after completion.

title: Waste Ink Bottle and Drain System Instructions
content:
The waste ink system collects ink discharged during cleaning, purging, and maintenance operations. Check
the waste ink bottle level every day before production.
If the bottle is nearly full, empty it safely into an approved waste container . Wear gloves while handling
waste ink. Avoid spilling waste ink near electrical components or walkways.
Ensure the waste ink tube is not bent, blocked, or disconnected. A blocked waste ink line may cause
overflow near the capping station.
Do not operate heavy cleaning cycles if the waste ink bottle is full. Overflow may damage nearby parts and
create unsafe working conditions.
Dispose of waste ink according to local environmental and company safety procedures.
Record waste ink disposal in the maintenance log if required.
8

title: General Operator Safety Instructions
content:
Only trained operators should use the textile printing machine. Before operation, the operator must
understand the control panel, emergency stop button, carriage movement area, and basic maintenance
points.
Do not place hands, tools, or fabric rolls inside the carriage movement area while the machine is active.
Loose clothing, ID cards, or cables should be kept away from moving parts.
Wear gloves when handling ink, cleaning solution, and waste ink. Use safety glasses if there is a risk of
splashing.
Do not open electrical panels while the machine is powered on. Electrical inspection must be performed
only by qualified personnel.
Keep the machine area clean and dry. Ink spills, fabric waste, and loose wires may cause slipping or tripping
hazards.
Report abnormal smell, smoke, sound, or vibration immediately.

title: Fabric Pre-Treatment Guidelines Before Printing
content:
Fabric pre-treatment improves ink bonding, color brightness, and wash durability. The correct pre-
treatment method depends on fabric type, ink type, and production requirement.
Before printing, ensure that the fabric is clean, dry, and free from oil, dust, and loose fibers. Pre-treated
fabric must be dried properly before loading onto the machine.
Uneven pre-treatment may cause patchy print output, poor color strength, or inconsistent wash results.
Excessive pre-treatment may make the fabric stiff or affect hand feel.
For dark garments, white ink performance depends strongly on proper pre-treatment. Always test new
fabric lots before bulk production.
Store pre-treated garments in a clean and dry area. Do not expose them to dust or moisture before
printing.
9

title: Print Quality Inspection After Printing
content:
After printing, inspect the output before approving bulk production. Check design placement, color
consistency, sharpness, opacity, banding, stains, and missing print areas.
Compare the print with the approved sample or digital reference. Minor color variation may occur
depending on fabric type, pre-treatment, curing, and lighting conditions.
If banding appears, perform a nozzle test and check print mode settings. If colors appear dull, check ink
level, white ink layer , curing condition, and fabric pre-treatment.
Do not continue bulk printing if defects are repeated in multiple pieces. Stop production and identify the
cause.
Approved samples and print settings should be stored for repeat orders. Inspection records help reduce
rework and customer complaints.

title: Basic Curing Instructions for Printed Fabric
content:
After printing, the fabric must be cured according to ink and fabric requirements. Curing helps fix the ink
onto the fabric and improves wash durability.
Use the recommended temperature, time, and pressure settings for the ink system. Insufficient curing may
cause poor wash fastness, cracking, or fading. Excessive heat may damage fabric, change hand feel, or
affect color .
Before bulk curing, test one sample and inspect the result. Check whether the print surface is dry, smooth,
and properly bonded.
Do not stack freshly printed garments before curing unless the process allows it. Wet ink may transfer to
other areas and cause stains.
Maintain curing equipment regularly and verify temperature accuracy. Incorrect curing temperature is a
common reason for print failure.
10

title: Printhead Not Firing During Nozzle Test
content:
If one or more printheads are not firing during the nozzle test, first check whether the ink tank has
sufficient ink. Low ink level may allow air to enter the ink line and cause firing failure.
Next, inspect the ink line for visible air bubbles. If bubbles are present, run ink circulation for three to five
minutes and repeat the nozzle test. If the issue continues, perform light cleaning followed by another
nozzle test.
Check whether the printhead cable is properly connected. A loose or damaged cable can prevent the
printhead from receiving firing signals. Do not remove or reconnect printhead cables while the machine is
powered on.
If the issue affects only one color channel, check that specific ink path, filter , and damper . If all channels are
not firing, check machine status, printhead voltage settings, software connection, and emergency stop
status.
Do not perform repeated heavy cleaning without identifying the cause. Excessive cleaning may waste ink
and reduce printhead life.

title: Horizontal Banding Appears in Print
content:
Banding appears as visible horizontal lines or uneven density across the printed image. The most common
causes are missing nozzles, incorrect print mode, poor fabric movement, or improper bed height.
Start by performing a nozzle test. If missing lines are visible, clean the affected printhead and repeat the
test. If the nozzle test is correct, check the selected print mode and pass count.
Low pass settings may increase speed but can make banding more visible. Use a higher quality mode for
detailed artwork, gradients, or premium samples.
Check whether the fabric is flat and properly held on the print bed. Wrinkles, loose fabric, or height
variation can affect ink placement.
If banding continues, inspect encoder strip cleanliness and carriage movement. Dust or ink mist on the
encoder strip may cause position errors.

title: Ink Leakage Around Printhead Area
content:
Ink leakage near the printhead may occur due to loose ink tube connections, damaged dampers, excessive
ink pressure, improper capping, or damaged seals.
Stop printing immediately if ink leakage is observed. Move the carriage to the maintenance position and
turn off printhead firing. Wipe leaked ink carefully using approved lint-free material.
Inspect the ink tubes and damper connections. Do not pull the tubes forcefully. If a tube is loose, it must be
secured before restarting production.
Check whether the capping station is overfilled with waste ink. A blocked waste ink path can cause ink to
accumulate near the printhead.
If leakage continues after basic inspection, stop the machine and inform service personnel. Printing with
leakage may damage the printhead, carriage board, or fabric.

title: Carriage Movement Error or Abnormal Carriage Stop
content:
A carriage movement error can occur when the carriage path is blocked, the encoder strip is dirty, the rail is
dry, or the motor cannot move smoothly.
First, press stop and check whether any fabric, tool, thread, or object is blocking the carriage path. Remove
the obstruction only after the machine has stopped.
Inspect the print bed height. If the fabric is too high, it may touch the printhead or carriage and trigger
movement errors.
Clean the encoder strip gently if dust or ink mist is visible. Do not scratch the strip. Check the linear rail and
apply approved lubrication if required.
Restart the machine and perform a slow manual carriage movement test. If the carriage makes unusual
noise or stops again, do not continue production. Report the issue for service inspection.

title: Encoder Strip Error During Printing
content:
The encoder strip helps the machine detect carriage position. If the encoder strip is dirty, scratched, loose,
or misread by the sensor , the machine may show position errors or print misalignment.
Stop the current print job and move the carriage to the service position. Inspect the encoder strip under
proper lighting. Look for ink mist, dust, scratches, or oil marks.
Clean the strip using approved solution and a soft lint-free wipe. Wipe gently in one direction. Do not use
sharp tools or rough cloth.
After cleaning, restart the machine and run a test print. If the same error appears again, check whether the
strip is properly seated and whether the encoder sensor is clean.
A damaged encoder strip should be replaced by trained service personnel.

title: White Ink Flow Is Weak or Interrupted
content:
White ink may stop flowing properly if it has settled, if the filter is blocked, if air is present in the ink line, or
if circulation is not working correctly.
Start by checking the white ink level. Ensure that the tank has enough ink and that the ink has not expired.
Run the white ink circulation system for five minutes.
Inspect the white ink line for air bubbles or sediment. If bubbles are visible, continue circulation and
perform a nozzle test. If the white nozzle test is weak, perform light cleaning.
Check the white ink filter and damper if flow remains poor . Blocked filters may restrict ink movement and
cause weak opacity.
Do not print on dark garments until white ink flow is stable. Poor white ink flow will cause dull colors,
uneven opacity, and rejected output.

title: Machine Software Cannot Detect Printer
content:
If the software does not detect the printer , first check whether the machine controller is powered on and
fully initialized. Confirm that the emergency stop button is released.
Check the USB, Ethernet, or communication cable based on the machine configuration. The cable should be
firmly connected and not damaged.
Restart the software once. If the printer is still not detected, restart the machine controller and then reopen
the software.
Verify that the correct printer driver or communication profile is selected in the software. If the machine
uses an IP address, confirm that the computer network settings are correct.
Do not reinstall software immediately without checking power , cable, driver , and network settings. If
detection fails after all checks, report the issue to technical support.

title: Printed Colors Are Misaligned or Shadowed
content:
Print misalignment may appear as color shadow, double image, blurred edges, or poor registration
between white and color layers.
First, confirm that the fabric is not moving during printing. Loose fabric or poor bed adhesion may cause
image shift.
Run the alignment test pattern from the software. If the lines do not overlap properly, adjust the alignment
values in small steps and repeat the test.
Check whether the correct print mode and machine profile are selected. Changing print mode without
alignment verification may cause visible registration differences.
Inspect the encoder strip and carriage movement if misalignment changes randomly between prints.
Mechanical looseness or encoder contamination can cause unstable alignment.
Save the corrected alignment settings after completing the test.

title: Low Air Pressure Warning on Machine Panel
content:
A low air pressure warning indicates that the machine is not receiving the required air pressure for stable
operation. Some machine functions may stop until pressure is restored.
Check the air compressor and confirm that it is switched on. Verify the pressure gauge reading and
compare it with the required operating range.
Inspect air tubes for leakage, bending, loose fittings, or disconnection. Listen for air leakage sounds near
connectors and valves.
Drain water from the air filter if moisture is visible. Excess moisture can affect pneumatic components and
reduce reliability.
Do not bypass the air pressure warning. Restart printing only after pressure returns to the acceptable range
and the warning is cleared.

title: Fabric Touches Printhead During Printing
content:
If fabric touches the printhead, stop the print immediately. Fabric contact may damage the nozzle surface
and cause print defects.
Check whether the fabric was loaded flat. Wrinkles, thick seams, collars, folded edges, or loose fabric parts
may rise above the print surface.
Lower the print bed slightly and move the carriage manually across the print area to confirm safe clearance.
Maintain enough gap between the printhead and the highest fabric point.
Inspect the printhead surface for lint, ink smearing, or physical damage after contact. Perform a nozzle test
before continuing production.
Do not continue printing if repeated contact occurs. Recheck fabric holding method, bed height, garment
placement, and print area clearance.
5

title: Printed Color Does Not Match Approved Sample
content:
Color mismatch may occur due to wrong color profile, different fabric type, incorrect ink limit, poor pre-
treatment, curing variation, or nozzle issues.
First, compare the current fabric with the approved sample fabric. Different fabric composition or surface
finish can change color appearance.
Check whether the correct print profile and print mode are selected. Using the wrong profile may cause
color shift or dull output.
Perform a nozzle test to confirm that all color channels are firing correctly. Missing nozzles may reduce
color strength or alter tone.
Check curing temperature and time. Under-curing or over-heating can affect final color and wash
performance.
For repeat orders, use saved print settings and approved sample references whenever available.

title: Print Fades or Cracks After Washing
content:
If the print fades, cracks, or washes out, curing may be insufficient or incorrect. Curing fixes the ink onto the
fabric and is critical for durability.
Check the curing equipment temperature using an independent temperature measurement method if
available. The displayed temperature may not always match actual surface temperature.
Verify curing time, pressure, and temperature against the ink system recommendation. Insufficient heat or
short curing time may result in poor wash fastness.
Check whether fabric pre-treatment was applied correctly. Uneven or poor pre-treatment can also reduce
durability.
Before bulk production, cure and wash-test one sample. Do not approve production until the sample passes
basic durability checks.
6

title: Printed Image Appears Blurry
content:
Blurry print output can be caused by incorrect bed height, poor artwork resolution, fabric movement,
printhead alignment error , or unsuitable print mode.
Check the artwork resolution before printing. Low-resolution images may appear soft or pixelated even if
the machine is working correctly.
Verify the bed height. If the gap between the printhead and fabric is too large, ink droplets may spread
before reaching the surface, causing misty or blurry output.
Check whether the fabric is flat and firmly held. Movement during printing can reduce sharpness.
Run print alignment if the blur appears as double edges or color shadow. Select a higher quality print mode
for fine text or detailed artwork.

title: Machine Does Not Start After Power On
content:
If the machine does not start, check the main power supply first. Confirm that the power cable is connected
and the main switch is turned on.
Check the emergency stop button. If it is pressed, release it according to the machine design before
restarting.
Verify that the power stabilizer or UPS is switched on and functioning. If there is no display on the control
panel, inspect the input power and fuse with qualified electrical support.
Do not open electrical panels without authorization. Electrical inspection must be done by trained
personnel.
If the control panel turns on but the software does not connect, follow the printer detection
troubleshooting procedure.

title: Emergency Stop Alarm Remains Active
content:
7
If the emergency stop alarm remains active, first check all emergency stop buttons on the machine. Some
machines may have more than one emergency stop location.
Ensure that each emergency stop button is fully released. Rotate or pull the button as per the machine
design.
Restart the machine controller after releasing the button. Open the software and check whether the alarm
has cleared.
If the alarm continues, inspect safety switches, door sensors, and emergency stop wiring. Do not bypass
safety circuits to continue production.
A persistent emergency stop alarm should be handled by service personnel. The machine should not be
operated until the safety system is working correctly.

title: Ink Tank Level Warning Appears
content:
An ink tank level warning means that the ink level is near or below the minimum operating level. Continuing
production with low ink may introduce air into the ink line.
Pause production and check the physical ink level in the tank. Refill using the correct approved ink for that
color channel.
After refilling, close the tank cap properly and clean any spilled ink. Run ink circulation for two to three
minutes to stabilize flow.
Perform a nozzle test before resuming production, especially if the tank was very low.
If the warning remains after refilling, check the ink level sensor or software status. Report the issue if the
sensor reading is incorrect.

title: Ink Smudges or Spreads on Fabric Surface
content:
Ink smudging may occur when the fabric is wet, pre-treatment is excessive, ink limit is too high, curing is
delayed, or the printhead touches the fabric.
Check whether the fabric is dry before printing. Moisture can cause ink to spread and reduce edge
sharpness.
Review the pre-treatment process. Excessive pre-treatment or uneven application can cause patchy
absorption and smudging.
Check print settings such as ink limit and number of passes. Over-inking may cause wet surface and slow
drying.
Inspect bed height and fabric flatness. If the printhead touches the fabric, it can drag wet ink and create
smudge marks.
Allow printed fabric to cure properly before stacking or handling.

title: Repeated Communication Error Between PC and Printer
content:
Repeated communication errors may be caused by loose cables, unstable power , incorrect network settings,
driver issues, or electrical noise.
Check the communication cable and replace it if damaged. Avoid running communication cables close to
heavy power cables or high-noise equipment.
If the printer uses Ethernet, confirm IP address, subnet, and network adapter settings. Disable unnecessary
network adapters if they interfere with printer communication.
Restart the software and controller in the correct order . Power instability may also cause communication
loss, so verify UPS or stabilizer condition.
Check the software log for repeated error codes. If errors occur at the same operation stage, report the
code to technical support.

title: Nozzles Missing After Machine Was Idle
content:
If the machine was idle for a long period, nozzles may dry or partially block. This is common when the
printhead was not capped properly or ink circulation was not maintained.
Check the capping station first. The printhead should be properly seated on the cap when idle.
Run ink circulation and perform a nozzle test. If only a few lines are missing, perform light cleaning and
repeat the test.
If many nozzles are missing, allow the printhead to soak using the approved maintenance procedure. Do
not scrape the nozzle plate.
To prevent drying, follow the shutdown procedure, maintain proper capping, and run regular circulation
during long idle periods.

title: Machine Shows Overheating Warning
content:
An overheating warning may appear when electronics, motors, or internal components exceed safe
operating temperature.
Stop active production and allow the machine to cool. Check whether cooling fans are running and whether
air vents are blocked by dust, fabric, or nearby objects.
Inspect the room temperature and ventilation. Long production runs in hot environments may increase
overheating risk.
Clean air filters if they are clogged. Poor airflow can cause repeated overheating warnings.
Do not ignore overheating alarms. Continuing production under overheating conditions may damage
electronic boards, motors, or power components.
If the warning appears frequently, report the issue for service inspection.
10

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What should be ready before machine installation?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Before the installation team arrives, the customer must ensure that the installation area is clean, level, and
free from unnecessary materials. The floor should be strong enough to support the machine weight and
allow safe operator movement around the printer .
The required power supply, grounding, air compressor connection, ventilation, and network access should
be prepared in advance. If the machine requires a stabilizer or UPS, it should be installed before machine
commissioning.
The customer should also arrange basic consumables such as approved ink, cleaning solution, lint-free
wipes, sample fabric, and pre-treatment material if applicable.
At least one operator and one maintenance person should be available during installation and training.
Installation may be delayed if power , air , space, or required consumables are not ready.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: Who should attend machine operator training?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Operator training should be attended by the person who will handle daily machine operation, print job
loading, fabric loading, nozzle testing, cleaning, and basic maintenance.
It is recommended that at least two people attend training. One person should be the primary operator ,
and the second person should understand backup operation and maintenance tasks.
Training usually covers startup, shutdown, nozzle test, ink filling, software job loading, safety points, print
quality checks, and daily cleaning. Advanced service-level repairs are not covered in basic operator training.
Operators should take notes during training and follow the provided checklist during the first few weeks of
production.
1

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What is usually covered under machine warranty?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Machine warranty generally covers manufacturing defects and failures of covered components during the
approved warranty period. The exact warranty terms depend on the commercial agreement, invoice, and
service policy.
Warranty may not cover damage caused by improper operation, unauthorized modification, use of
unapproved ink, poor power supply, physical damage, negligence, or lack of maintenance.
Consumables such as ink, filters, dampers, wiper blades, cleaning solution, and waste ink parts may have
limited or no warranty coverage depending on usage.
The customer should maintain operation records, maintenance logs, and service reports. Warranty claims
may require photographs, error details, usage history, and inspection by authorized service personnel.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: How to raise a service request?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
To raise a service request, the customer should share the machine model, serial number , location, contact
person, issue description, error message, and recent activity before the issue occurred.
Clear photos or videos should be shared whenever possible. For print quality issues, include the nozzle test
result, sample print image, print settings, fabric type, and ink details.
The support team will review the information and may provide remote troubleshooting steps first. If the
issue cannot be resolved remotely, a service visit may be scheduled.
Customers should not dismantle critical parts unless instructed by authorized service personnel.
Unauthorized repair attempts may increase damage and affect warranty eligibility.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What information is required for remote support?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
For remote support, the customer should provide a clear description of the issue, machine model, software
version, error message, and when the problem started.
If the issue is related to printing, the customer should provide photos of the printed output, nozzle test,
fabric type, ink type, print mode, and recent changes in settings.
If the issue is related to software detection or communication, the customer should share screenshots of
the software status, cable connection, network settings, and any error logs.
Remote support is most effective when the operator is available near the machine and can perform
instructed checks safely.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: Why is preventive maintenance important?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Preventive maintenance helps reduce unexpected downtime, print defects, ink blockage, component wear ,
and emergency service calls.
Daily cleaning prevents dust, lint, dried ink, and fabric waste from affecting print quality or carriage
movement. Weekly inspection helps identify loose cables, air leaks, weak ink flow, and abnormal sounds
early.
Maintenance should not be skipped during low production periods. Idle machines can still develop
printhead drying, ink settling, and capping problems.
A maintenance log should be maintained to record cleaning, ink refilling, nozzle test results, filter changes,
and service observations. This helps both the customer and service team diagnose repeated issues faster .

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: Why should only approved ink be used?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Approved ink is tested for compatibility with the machine ink system, printhead, filters, dampers, curing
conditions, and fabric output requirements.
Using unapproved ink may cause nozzle blockage, color inconsistency, sediment formation, weak wash
durability, filter clogging, or printhead damage.
Different ink brands should not be mixed in the same tank unless approved by the manufacturer . Even if
two inks appear similar , their chemistry may be different.
If a customer wants to change ink type or supplier , the support team should be informed before switching.
Proper flushing and validation may be required before using a new ink system.
3

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: Which spare parts should be kept in stock?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Customers running regular production should keep basic consumable and maintenance spares available to
avoid downtime. Recommended items include filters, dampers, wiper blades, capping station parts,
cleaning solution, lint-free wipes, waste ink tubes, and approved ink.
For high-volume production, additional critical parts may be recommended based on machine model and
usage. These may include sensors, belts, cables, pumps, and pneumatic fittings.
Printheads are high-value components and should be stored and handled only as per service guidelines.
The exact spare list should be confirmed with the support team based on the machine configuration,
production volume, and service location.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: Should the machine software be updated regularly?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Software updates may include bug fixes, performance improvements, new print modes, improved machine
communication, or updated safety checks.
Customers should not install unofficial software versions or modify machine software files without
approval. Incorrect software versions may cause communication errors, wrong print settings, or machine
instability.
Before updating software, backup important profiles, print settings, alignment values, and job
configurations. The support team may request current software version details before recommending an
update.
After software update, perform a test print and verify machine status, profile selection, alignment, and print
quality before resuming bulk production.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What site conditions are recommended for stable operation?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
4
The machine should be installed in a clean, dry, and dust-controlled area. Excessive dust, fabric lint,
moisture, or heat may affect machine reliability and print quality.
The room should have sufficient ventilation and stable temperature. Ink, fabric, and machine electronics
may behave differently in extreme conditions.
Power supply should be stable, properly grounded, and protected from sudden fluctuations. Air supply
should be clean, dry, and within the required pressure range.
The machine should have enough space around it for loading fabric, maintenance access, operator
movement, and emergency access.
Good site conditions reduce downtime and improve output consistency.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What should the operator check before starting production?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Before production, the operator should check machine power , air pressure, ink levels, fabric readiness, print
bed cleanliness, software status, and emergency stop condition.
A nozzle test should be performed before starting production. If missing nozzles are found, cleaning should
be done before printing customer orders.
The operator should confirm that the correct design file, print profile, print mode, fabric type, and print
origin are selected.
For repeat jobs, compare the setup with the approved sample and saved settings. For new jobs, a sample
print should be approved before bulk printing.
Production should not begin if the machine shows active alarms or if the printhead may contact the fabric.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What should be done at the end of the day?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
At the end of the day, complete all active print jobs and move the carriage to the home or capping position.
Do not leave the printhead exposed.
Run the recommended ink circulation or maintenance cycle if required. Clean the printhead surface,
capping station, wiper area, print bed, and waste ink area.
Check the waste ink bottle and empty it if needed. Record ink usage, maintenance activity, production
issues, and nozzle test condition.
Shut down the software and machine according to the standard shutdown procedure. Do not disconnect
power abruptly unless there is an emergency.
A proper end-of-day routine reduces nozzle drying and startup issues on the next working day.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What details are needed for a print quality complaint?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
For a print quality complaint, the customer should share the approved sample, current defective print,
nozzle test result, design file information, fabric type, ink details, print mode, curing method, and
production date.
The support team may ask whether the issue appears on all prints or only on specific garments. This helps
separate machine issues from fabric, pre-treatment, or handling issues.
Photos should be taken under proper lighting. For banding, blur , smudging, or color mismatch, close-up
images are useful.
Customers should not continue bulk production if the defect repeats. Stop production, keep sample
evidence, and contact support for guidance.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What should be done if the machine will remain idle?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
If the machine will remain idle for a short period, ensure that the printhead is capped properly and that the
required circulation cycle is performed.
For longer idle periods, follow the manufacturer-recommended storage or shutdown procedure. This may
include cleaning, flushing, capping, circulation scheduling, and environmental precautions.
The machine area should remain clean and protected from dust. Ink tanks should be closed properly to
avoid contamination.
Before restarting after idle time, inspect ink levels, run circulation, perform a nozzle test, and check print
quality with a test print.
Improper idle handling may cause nozzle drying, ink settling, weak flow, or startup failures.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: How should ink and cleaning consumables be stored?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
Ink, cleaning solution, wipes, filters, and other consumables should be stored in a clean and dry area away
from direct sunlight, dust, and extreme temperature.
Ink containers should remain tightly closed when not in use. Before filling ink, check the label, color , batch
information, and expiry date if available.
Do not store consumables near heat sources, open electrical panels, or contaminated production waste.
Lint-free wipes and cleaning materials should be kept sealed to prevent dust contamination. Dirty cleaning
material can damage the printhead or introduce particles into the ink system.
Expired or contaminated consumables should not be used.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: When should an issue be escalated to service personnel?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
An issue should be escalated when basic operator checks do not resolve the problem, when the same error
repeats, or when there is risk of machine damage or operator injury.
Examples requiring escalation include ink leakage near electronics, repeated carriage errors, emergency
stop faults, burning smell, smoke, electrical issues, printhead collision, and unresolved communication
errors.
Operators should also escalate if they are unsure about a maintenance step. Guesswork or unauthorized
repair can increase damage.
Before escalation, collect relevant details such as machine status, error message, photos, recent
maintenance, and steps already attempted.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: Why should machine logs and maintenance records be maintained?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
7
Logs help track machine behavior , maintenance history, consumable usage, print quality issues, and
repeated errors. Good records allow faster troubleshooting and better service decisions.
Operators should record daily cleaning, nozzle test results, ink refill dates, waste ink disposal, filter changes,
software errors, and major production issues.
For recurring problems, logs help identify patterns such as problems after idle periods, specific fabric types,
power fluctuations, or particular print modes.
Service teams may request logs before remote or on-site diagnosis. Missing records can make
troubleshooting slower and less accurate.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: What are the customer responsibilities after installation?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
After installation, the customer is responsible for daily operation, cleaning, safe handling, approved
consumable usage, site cleanliness, and basic maintenance according to the provided instructions.
The customer should ensure that only trained operators use the machine. Operators should not bypass
alarms, modify settings randomly, or perform unauthorized repairs.
The customer must maintain proper power , air supply, ventilation, and environmental conditions.
Any abnormal sound, smell, leakage, print defect, or alarm should be reported early. Continuing production
despite warnings may increase damage and downtime.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: How should repeat orders be handled?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
For repeat orders, use the previously approved design file, print profile, print mode, ink settings, fabric type,
curing settings, and sample reference wherever possible.
Before starting bulk production, print one sample and compare it with the approved sample. Check
placement, color , opacity, sharpness, and curing result.
If fabric batch, ink batch, pre-treatment, or curing method has changed, small variation may occur .
Adjustments should be documented.
Do not assume that repeat orders will match automatically if production conditions have changed. Maintain
saved settings and production notes for consistency.

dataset_name: Synthetic Dataset 3: Support Documents and FAQs
title: How to communicate effectively with support team?
source_pdf: Synthetic Dataset 3- Support Documents and FAQs.pdf
content:
When contacting support, provide complete and clear information. Mention the machine model, serial
number , location, operator name, contact number , issue description, and urgency.
For error messages, share screenshots or exact wording. For print defects, share clear photos of the defect,
nozzle test, sample print, fabric, and settings.
Explain what changed recently, such as new ink, new fabric, software update, power issue, maintenance
activity, or long idle period.
Avoid sending only short messages such as “machine not working” or “print problem.” Incomplete
information may delay diagnosis.
Follow support instructions carefully and confirm the result after each troubleshooting step.
9
